title: TPU v1 · Manufacturability and Reliability Signoff: DFM, ESD/Latch-Up, and Aging
oneLine: Clean function, timing, power, and DRC are not enough. Signoff must also prove acceptable manufacturing yield and reliable 24×7 service over years. DFM improves redundant-via and lithographic robustness; ESD/latch-up checks protect assembly and transients; aging-aware analysis covers HCI, NBTI, and EM at end of life. All three feed a yield + lifetime gate. TPU's private yield and reliability values remain undisclosed.

legend.name: Converged GDSII under signoff
legend.desc: A large 28 nm, ≤331 mm² die is sensitive to random defects and systematic process variation, while continuous data-center inference demands years of service. Public TPU architecture supplies context; actual yield and reliability reports are not public.

legend.name: DFM signoff (redundant vias / lithography / yield)
legend.desc: DRC legality does not guarantee high yield. Foundry DFM decks evaluate redundant-via coverage, CMP fill and pattern regularity, lithographic hotspots, critical spacing, and yield prediction. Illustrative acceptance requires target via coverage, no unwaived hotspot, and project DFM/yield score.

legend.name: ESD / latch-up protection signoff
legend.desc: Verify every I/O has an adequate ESD clamp and complete discharge path, and that well/substrate taps and spacing suppress parasitic PNPN latch-up. ESD or latch-up failure can destroy a part during assembly, power-up, or a transient. Exact TPU protection is unpublished.

legend.name: Aging signoff (HCI / NBTI / EM)
legend.desc: HCI and NBTI shift transistor thresholds and slow gates over time; EM degrades high-current metal. Aging-aware STA and foundry degradation models must show positive end-of-life timing and EM margin under hot, high-activity full-array use.

legend.name: Yield + lifetime gate
legend.desc: Aggregate DFM, ESD/latch-up, and aging. The chip must be manufacturable at acceptable yield and survive continuous service to its design lifetime. All three dimensions must pass; exact TPU targets and thresholds are private.

steps.cap: ① Evaluate the converged large-die layout for two risks: yield sensitivity at 28 nm/≤331 mm² and long-term degradation under 24×7 data-center service.
② Run DFM: add redundant vias, regularize/fill patterns, fix lithographic hotspots, and meet illustrative via-coverage and yield-prediction gates.
③ Run ESD/latch-up checks: verify I/O clamps, discharge paths, and well/substrate taps; zero unapproved protection-rule violations is a hard criterion.
④ Run aging-aware HCI/NBTI/EM analysis at end of life and full-load activity, requiring nonnegative path slack and compliant EM lifetime margin.
⑤ Pass the yield + lifetime gate only when DFM, ESD/latch-up, and aging are green; otherwise return to ⑧ ECO and recheck.

before: Define DFM runsets and yield criteria: Agree on foundry 28 nm redundant-via coverage, CMP density, lithographic-hotspot, printability, and yield-score requirements. DFM optimizes robustness; DRC only establishes legality.
Define ESD/latch-up protection requirements: Set HBM/CDM clamp targets, discharge paths, well/substrate tap density, and I/O-to-core spacing. Illustratively, protection-rule violations must be zero.

Define aging models, lifetime, and stress: Select foundry HCI/NBTI and EM models, the continuous-service target, temperature, and activity. Evaluate the fully active array as a high-stress case and require positive end-of-life timing/EM margin.

Fix golden tools and waiver governance: Use Calibre-class DFM/LFD, layout-level ESD/latch-up checks, aging-aware STA, and RedHawk/Voltus-class EM. Define who may approve exceptions and how evidence is recorded.
Keep the objective distinct: This stage is not rechecking immediate functionality. It asks whether the design can be produced at acceptable yield and continue operating reliably over years.
during: Core rule: DFM passing is not DRC passing. A legal large die full of single vias or lithography-sensitive patterns may still yield poorly. DFM proactively hardens the layout with via redundancy, fill, and hotspot repair.
ESD and latch-up are non-negotiable survival gates. Inadequate clamps, discharge paths, or taps can kill a chip before normal service. Clear every issue or retain only formally approved waivers.

Analyze end-of-life, full-load conditions—not day zero. Add HCI/NBTI delay degradation and EM lifetime stress under hot, high-activity operation. A fresh chip's clean timing does not guarantee years of 24×7 service.
The three checks cover different failure modes. DFM addresses yield, ESD/latch-up transient survival, and aging long-term degradation. One green result cannot substitute for another.

Signoff exposes issues; backend creates margin. Return insufficient via redundancy, missing protection, or negative EOL timing to ⑧ for added vias, clamps/taps, or reduced stress. The signoff team preserves criteria.

Disclosure boundary: DFM, ESD/latch-up, and HCI/NBTI/EM aging are standard large-ASIC signoff. TPU's via coverage, ESD ratings, lifetime target, and margins are unpublished; all criteria shown are illustrative.

result.label: Signoff proposition
result.value: Manufacturable at acceptable yield + reliable for years of 24×7 data-center operation

result.label: DFM signoff
result.value: Redundant vias / fill / lithographic hotspots / yield optimization; illustrative coverage and yield gates

result.label: ESD / latch-up signoff
result.value: I/O clamps and discharge paths + well/substrate taps; zero unapproved rule violations

result.label: Aging signoff
result.value: End-of-life HCI/NBTI delay + EM under full load; path slack ≥0 and EM lifetime margin positive

result.label: Aggregate gate
result.value: All three pass the yield + lifetime gate before ⑩ fabrication

result.label: Release / reject
result.value: Pass → release; failure → ⑧ ECO for vias, ESD/taps, or stress margin → recheck

after: Return each failure to targeted ⑧ ECO: Add redundant vias/fill or fix lithography for DFM; add clamps and taps for protection; add timing/metal margin or reduce stress for aging.

Recheck affected and coupled dimensions: Vias/fill alter parasitics and density, protection devices alter I/O layout, and aging margin affects timing. Re-extract and ensure no regression in STA, IR, or DRC.
Apply the yield + lifetime gate: Compare DFM, ESD/latch-up, and aging reports with predefined criteria. Any red or unreviewed exception blocks release.
Feed the overall tapeout gate: Reliability/manufacturability joins STA, EM/IR, DRC/LVS, LEC, and power. Only an all-green GDSII proceeds to fabrication.
Archive traceable reliability evidence: Preserve DFM/yield, ESD/latch-up, EOL aging reports, runsets, models, and waivers for post-silicon yield or early-life failure investigations.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (published TPU v1 array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 700 MHz, 28 nm, ≤331 mm², 28–40 W, weight-stationary dataflow, and data-center inference role provide context. Redundant-via/lithography/yield DFM, ESD/latch-up checks, HCI/NBTI/EM aging, and yield+lifetime governance are standard illustrative signoff practices, not private TPU yield, reliability, ESD, lifetime, or margin data.)
